opening: The dice know what you're afraid of.
You come to the table. Let's see what you brought.
Most people fight luck. Then they're surprised when it fights back.
Pay attention. Not to the numbers. To yourself.
Before you roll, know what you're willing to lose.
Luck is not random. It follows the spirit.
Everyone thinks they deserve the good numbers. That's not how it works.
The dice don't lie. Most people can't say the same.
Patience is not waiting. Patience is knowing what you're waiting for.
There are things you control. There are things you don't. Most people mix them up.
I've watched hawks hunt. They don't talk themselves into it first.
You will make choices here. They will say something about you.
Some people have the right spirit for this. Some grow into it. Some never do.
Don't come to the table fighting. You'll lose before the first roll.
The number doesn't matter as much as what you do next.
Every game starts the same. That's the only thing that's equal.
You already know more than you think you do. The problem is listening to the wrong part.
Some people come to the table ready. Most people just come.
You are not owed anything here.
Watch what you reach for. That tells you more than any roll.
Every choice closes a door. Know which doors you're closing.
The nervous ones always play fast. Slow down.
I've seen people pray over dice. The dice don't care.
You will want to take back a roll today. You won't be able to.
The wrong move is usually obvious. The right one takes longer.
Some rounds you have nothing. What you do with nothing is the question.
People who blame luck usually made a decision they don't want to look at.
Be honest about what you have. The dice already know.
A good player knows what to do with bad rolls. Anyone can handle good ones.
This game doesn't lie about you. Whether you want to know what it says is your business.
Hesitation is expensive. So is rushing.
You can't control what you're given. You can control what you do with it. That's everything.
The dice remember the last time you played. Do you?

really_bad: You were fighting the whole time. What were you fighting?
There's a difference between risk and waste. You'll learn it eventually.
The dice were not your problem today.
You spent your luck on the wrong things. That would be fine if you knew it was a choice.
You were not paying attention to what mattered.
You tried without understanding. That's not brave. That's just expensive.
Some things you have to see fail before you know why they fail. Now you've seen it.
I've seen worse. You've also done better.
You lost this before you started. You just didn't know it yet.
What you were doing wasn't working. You kept doing it.
You were hoping. Hoping is not a plan.
Somewhere in the middle of that game you knew. You didn't stop.
You played scared. Scared is expensive.
You threw away what you had chasing what you wanted. That never works.
Some scores are honest. This one is honest about something you need to look at.
I watched you make the same mistake more than once. That's the part I don't understand.
bad: The dice were difficult. You made it harder.
It wasn't all your fault. But some of it was.
Hard days come. The question is what you do inside them.
The dice were stubborn. You were more stubborn.
Bad luck is real. Don't let it cover up the choices that were yours.
You fought the table instead of working with it. I understand why. It still cost you.
Not every game teaches you something useful.
You needed the dice to rescue you. They didn't.
The dice gave you something to work with. You didn't use it.
You had moments. You didn't hold onto them.
You rushed something. I could see it.
The mistakes were small. Small mistakes add up to this.
You did not play your worst. This is not your best.
Some days the dice speak a language you can't hear. That was today.
You know what you did wrong. You don't need me to say it.
It could have gone either way. It went the wrong way.
ok: You made decisions I wouldn't have made. Some of them worked.
I've seen better from you. I've also seen worse.
You stayed in it. That counts for something.
The dice were neither generous nor cruel. Neither were you.
Middle of the road isn't failure. Don't celebrate it either.
You got out what you put in. Which was not nothing.
You walked out with something. Not everything, but something.
Nobody will remember this game. That's not necessarily a bad thing.
You held it together when it could have fallen apart. I'll give you that.
You played cautiously. Sometimes that's right. Today it was just cautious.
This is what competent looks like. It's enough.
The dice gave you an average day. You gave them one back.
You didn't waste it. You also didn't make the most of it.
good: You knew what to do and you did it. Good.
The spirit was right. The dice followed.
You didn't force anything. That's harder than it sounds.
You stopped when you should have stopped. I noticed.
You respected what you had. That matters more than people think.
Not everyone can read the table. You read it.
Some people would have blown this. You didn't.
You played with your whole mind. I could see it.
You made the hard choice at least twice. I noticed.
That was disciplined. Discipline is harder than talent.
You trusted yourself. That's not nothing.
The dice rewarded patience. They usually do.
You didn't panic when it got difficult. That matters.
You kept your head. I've seen people with better rolls make less of them.
I wouldn't change much about that game.
great: The dice were respected. They gave back.
You played like you had nothing to prove. That's the only way to play.
I've watched a lot of games. This was one of the good ones.
You didn't fight it. You didn't beg it. You just played.
Some people play a long time before they play like that. Some never do.
The dice were generous because you didn't demand it.
That's what it looks like when someone knows what they're doing.
Good. Now don't forget how that felt.
You made that look easy. It wasn't.
I've watched people play for a long time. This is what it's supposed to look like.
The dice gave you what you deserved. That's rare.
You never chased anything. Everything came from what you built.
That's what a clear head does.
Other people would have gotten less from those same rolls. That's the difference.
You played like you trusted yourself. You should.
That was clean from start to finish.

sixzee: There it is.
All five. The dice said yes.
That doesn't happen often. Remember how you were sitting.
Some people play their whole lives and never see that.
The dice were in a good mood. So were you.
Don't say anything. Just take it.
That's what the right spirit looks like.
Five the same. I've seen it before. Not often.
Hm. All five.
The dice decided you deserved that. Maybe they were right.
Some things you don't explain. You just accept them.
That's yours. Take it.
Five the same. Even I have to stop and look at that.
The universe was paying attention.
Once in a long while, the dice agree on something. Today they agreed on you.
That's a rare thing. Treat it like one.
I've seen people roll for years chasing that. You got it.
All five. The table knows.
The dice lined up for you. Don't make a show of it.
That's the game saying something. Listen.
Five the same. Accept it quietly.
That's not something you ask for. It just comes. And it came.
Even the dice have to agree sometimes. Today they did.
You were in the right place with the right spirit. It shows.
scratch: Sometimes you burn something down so it doesn't burn you.
You let it go. Not everyone can do that.
Knowing when to stop is its own kind of win.
A scratch isn't losing. It's accepting what's already true.
You gave that round up. Good. It was already gone.
Not everything can be saved. You knew that.
Walk away from what you can't use. That takes something.
Some rounds you just survive.
You cut it. That took a clear head.
That was the right choice. It didn't feel like it. It was.
You didn't fight for something that wasn't worth fighting for. Good.
Sometimes survival is the score.
You recognized a lost cause. That's a skill.
Letting go when it costs you something — that's harder than holding on.
You didn't throw good after bad. Smart.
That round had nothing for you. You saw it.
There is no shame in a scratch. There's only shame in pretending it wasn't one.
You took the loss clean. Good.
Some things are already decided before you realize it. You realized it.
That was the mature choice. I mean that.
Cutting your losses is not losing. It's reading.
The round was over. You accepted it.
You knew. You didn't fight it. That's wisdom.
You weren't going to win that round. You limited the damage. That's something.
quit: You decided you were done. That's a decision.
Not every game is worth finishing.
Walking away is a choice. You made one.
Some games aren't worth the rest of them.
You knew when you were done. That's not nothing.
Done is done. No shame in naming it.
You saw enough. You left.
Sometimes the honest thing is to stop.
You let the game go. Some people hold on long past knowing.
That's done now. So is the arguing with it.
The dice don't care. You don't have to either.
You chose. Most people don't admit they're choosing.
Finishing isn't always the point.
You saw where it was going. That counts.
Not every round earns the rest of it.
You stopped when you decided to stop. That's clarity.
The game doesn't hold it against you.
It wasn't working. You noticed. Both of those are harder than they sound.
Quitting and giving up aren't the same thing. You quit.
Some hands aren't worth playing to the end.
There's no honor in suffering through something you've already decided.
You knew. Acting on it is the hard part.
The game will still be here. You made a choice.
Sometimes walking away is the honest thing.
You were done. Now you're saying so.
